coredata.dc:title: Octadecylimidazolium ionic liquids-functionalized carbon dots and their precursor co-immobilized silica as hydrophobic chromatographic stationary phase with enhanced shape selectivity
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Danni
Chen, Jia
Guan, Ming
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, 1-vinyl-3-octadecylimidazolium bromide ionic liquids ([C18VIm]Br) and their derived carbon dots (ImC18CDs) were prepared, [C18VIm]Br and ImC18CDs were grafted on the silica to obtain Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18CDs, respectively, and they were also co-grafted on silica which named Sil-ImC18/CDs. Compared with Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18CDs columns, Sil-ImC18/CDs column exhibited enhanced selectivity for separation of tetracyclic/tricyclic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) isomers, and butylbenzene isomers in reversed-phase liquid chromatography, which may be due to the synergistic effect between ImC18CDs and [C18VIm]Br, the π-π interaction between imidazolium and analytes, etc. Meanwhile, the retention behavior of Sil-ImC18/CDs was further evaluated and compared with the commercial C18 column using different classes of analytes, including standard test mixtures of Tanaka, Engelhardt, SRM869b, SRM870. The results demonstrated that co-grafted column exhibited superior separation performance. And this column was applied to determine the contents of calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin in the extract of Radix Astragali, which were found that the concentration was 0.25 mg mL−1, 0.15 mg mL−1, 0.13 mg mL−1 and 0.30 mg mL−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ionic liquids
Stationary phase
Chromatographic separation
Selectivity enhancement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18 stationary phases
SRM870 and SRM869b standard test mixture
Separation of amines, phenols, PAHs and alkylbenzenes
Quantitative detection of flavonoids in Radix Astragali extract solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation for Sci & Tech Research Project of Gansu Province
Science and Technology Project of Science and Technology Bureau of Chengguan District in Lanzhou City
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Youth Innovation Promotion Association of the Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074154 and 21822407 ), Science and Technology Project of Science and Technology Bureau of Chengguan District in Lanzhou City ( 2020JSCX0033 ), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS ( 2021420 ) and the Foundation for Sci & Tech Research Project of Gansu Province ( 20JR10RA045 and 20JR5RA573 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation schematic of ImC18CDs, Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18/CDs and Sil-ImC18CDs.
TEM image of (a) ImC18CDs, LSCM image of (b) Sil-ImC18/CDs, contact angle analysis results of (c) Sil-MPS and (d) Sil-ImC18/CDs.

Chromatograms of the Tanaka standard test mixture, Engelhardt standard test mixture and phenols on Sil-ImC18/CDs (red lines) and ZORBAX SB-C18 (blue lines) columns. (a) Tanaka standard test mixture: (1) uracil, (2) caffeine, (3) phenol, (4) n-butylbenzene, (5) n-pentylbenzene, (6) o-terphenyl, (7) triphenylene. (b) Engelhardt standard test mixture: (1) thiourea, (2) aniline, (3) p-methylaniline, (4) toluene, (5) phenol, (6) ethylbenzene; mobile phase: methanol-water (55/45, v/v). (c) phenols: (1) p-aminophenol, (2) hydroquinone, (3) 2-aminophenol, (4) phenol, (5) p-cresol, (6) 2-acetaminophen, (7) 2-tert-butylphenol, (8) 2-naphthol. Sil-ImC18/CDs: mobile phase: (a) methanol-water (60/40, v/v), (b) methanol-water (55/45, v/v), (c) methanol-water (65/35, v/v); column temperature: (a) 30 °C, (b–c) 35 °C. ZORBAX SB-C18: mobile phase: (a) methanol-water (80/20, v/v), (b) methanol-water (55/45, v/v), (c) methanol-water (65/35, v/v); column temperature: (a) 30 °C, (b–c) 35 °C. Flow rate: (a–c) 1.0 mL min−1; UV detection wavelength: (a–c) 254 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Chromatograms of the PAHs and butylbenzene isomers on Sil-ImC18/CDs (red lines), Sil-ImC18CDs (purple lines), Sil-ImC18 (black lines) and ZORBAX SB-C18 (blue lines) columns. (a) Tetracyclic PAHs: (1) fluoranthene, (2) pyrene, (3) chrysene, (4) 1,2-benzanthracene. (b) Tricyclic PAHs analytes: (1) acenaphthene, (2) fluorene, (3) phenanthrene, (4) anthracene. (c) Butylbenzene isomer: (1) tert-butylbenzene, (2) sec-butylbenzene, (3) iso-butylbenzene, (4) n-butylbenzene. Sil-ImC18/CDs: mobile phase: (a) methanol-water (70/30, v/v); (b–c) methanol-water (65/35, v/v); column temperature: 30 °C. ZORBAX SB-C18: mobile phase: (a) methanol-water (70/30, v/v); (b–c) methanol-water (65/35, v/v); column temperature: 30 °C. Sil-ImC18CDs: (a–c) methanol-water (60/40, v/v); column temperature: 35 °C. Sil-ImC18: (a–c) methanol-water (90/10, v/v); column temperature: 35 °C. Flow rate: (a–c) 1.0 mL min−1; UV detection wavelength: (a–c) 254 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Standard curve of ononin, calycosin-7-glucoside, formononetin and calycosin. (b) Chromatograms of the separation of ononin, calycosin-7-glucoside, formononetin and calycosin standard and actual sample. Mobile phase: methanol-0.3% formic acid solution (0–30 min, 10%→70% methanol, 90%→30% 0.3% formic acid solution); flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1; column temperature: 30 °C; UV detection wavelength: 254 nm.
The results of elemental analysis of ImC18CDs, Sil-MPS, Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18/CDs materials.

head.ce:title: Octadecylimidazolium ionic liquids-functionalized carbon dots and their precursor co-immobilized silica as hydrophobic chromatographic stationary phase with enhanced shape selectivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinjiang Normal University, Urumqi, 830054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory on Pollution Monitoring and Control
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, 1-vinyl-3-octadecylimidazolium bromide ionic liquids ([C18VIm]Br) and their derived carbon dots (ImC18CDs) were prepared, [C18VIm]Br and ImC18CDs were grafted on the silica to obtain Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18CDs, respectively, and they were also co-grafted on silica which named Sil-ImC18/CDs. Compared with Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18CDs columns, Sil-ImC18/CDs column exhibited enhanced selectivity for separation of tetracyclic/tricyclic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) isomers, and butylbenzene isomers in reversed-phase liquid chromatography, which may be due to the synergistic effect between ImC18CDs and [C18VIm]Br, the π-π interaction between imidazolium and analytes, etc. Meanwhile, the retention behavior of Sil-ImC18/CDs was further evaluated and compared with the commercial C18 column using different classes of analytes, including standard test mixtures of Tanaka, Engelhardt, SRM869b, SRM870. The results demonstrated that co-grafted column exhibited superior separation performance. And this column was applied to determine the contents of calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin in the extract of Radix Astragali, which were found that the concentration was 0.25 mg mL−1, 0.15 mg mL−1, 0.13 mg mL−1 and 0.30 mg mL−1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) has become one of the most popular separation tools due to its fast analysis, good selectivity and easy operation. HPLC is widely used in the fields of biological analysis, environmental monitoring, chemical engineering and food safety [1–5]. The separation modes of HPLC include normal phase, reversed-phase, ion exchange, hydrophilic interaction and size exclusion. Generally speaking, the stationary phases modified with different functional molecules or materials can be applied in different chromatographic separation modes [6,7]. Recently, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [8], ionic liquids (ILs) [9,10], graphene [11] and carbon dots (CDs) [12] are modified on the surface of silica and used as novel chromatographic stationary phases.

Among which, CDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials, which have good stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and modifiability [13–15]. It plays an important role in fluorescence sensing [16–18], biological imaging [19,20], optoelectronic device [21], catalysis [22], chromatographic separation [23–25] and so on [26]. In our previous work, it was proved that CDs, whether directly used as functional modifiers or mixed with precursors, exhibited good separation performance for polar analytes [27–29]. However, until now, most of the reported CDs modified silica were used in hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC), few CDs-modified silica stationary phases reported for mixed-mode liquid chromatography [23].

As a new kind of chemically stable green solvents or functionalized materials, ionic liquids (ILs) are widely used in many different research areas [30,31]. In this work, octadecylimidazolium ionic liquids-derived CDs (ImC18CDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method using 1-vinyl-3-octadecylimidazolium bromide ILs ([C18VIm]Br) as carbon source. [C18VIm]Br and ImC18CDs were grafted on silica homogeneously to obtain Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18CDs, respectively. Also, they were co-grafted on silica to obtain Sil-ImC18/CDs for reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) (see Fig. 1). A variety of hydrophobic compounds including Tanaka, Engelhardt, SRM870, SRM869b, PAHs, alkylbenzenes and so on were chosen to test the separation performances. It was found that Sil-ImC18/CDs have superior selectivity than Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18CDs columns. And the content of calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin in Radix Astragali extract were quantitatively analyzed by Sil-ImC18/CDs column.

Spherical porous silica (diameter: 5 μm, pore diameter: 90 Å, specific surface area: 306 m2/g) was purchased from Fuji Silicon Chemical Co., Ltd. (Aichi, Japan). Dialysis bag (1000 Da) was bought in Beijing Solarbao Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Phosphoric acid (85%) was obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Sichuan, China). Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 98%) was purchased from Yuefeng Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Toluene and ethanol were bought from Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Methanol (chromatographic pure), acetonitrile (chromatographic pure), ammonium acetate (99%), (3-mercaptopropyl) triethoxysilane (γ-MPS, 96%) and other standard samples used for chromatographic separation (alkylbenzene, PAHs, aromatic amines, phenols) were gotten from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ononin, calycosin, formononetin and calycosin-7-glucoside were purchased from Push Bio-technology Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Radix Astragali was produced in Gansu, China.

Chromatographic separation was performed by using an Agilent 1260 Infinity Series modular system equipped with an injection valve, a G1311C quaternary pump, a diode array detector and a 20 μL sample loop (Agilent Technologies, USA). Transmission electron microscopies (TEM) imaging were obtained from Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging were performed on Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope FV1200 (Olympus, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on an IFS 120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Elemental analysis results were measured on aVario EL III element analyzer (Hanau, Germany).

ImC18CDs were prepared according to a modified method as reported in the literature [32]. 2.0 g of [C18VIm]Br was dissolved in certain amount of a phosphoric acid-ethanol solution (5 M phosphoric acid). The mixed-solution was sealed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 230 °C for 24 h. Then, the pH value was adjusted to neutral after falling to room temperature by using sodium hydroxide solution (1 M) dissolved in ethanol. The supernatant was collected and condensed by rotary evaporation and then dialyzed against water using cellulose ester membrane (1000 Da) for 48 h. The crude product precipitate from water, and it was separated by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, ImC18CDs was obtained after drying at 60 °C.

Firstly, 3.0 g of silica was dispersed into 20 mL of anhydrous toluene and 2 mL of γ-MPS was added into the mixed solution. Then, the suspension was mechanically stirring at 110 °C for 24 h under N2 atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, the product was centrifuged and washed with anhydrous toluene, methanol, ethanol, distilled water, and ethanol in this order. Finally, MPS-modified silica (Sil-MPS) was dried at 60 °C.

0.4 g of [C18VIm]Br, 0.8 g of ImC18CDs and 2.7 g of Sil-MPS were dispersed into 20 mL anhydrous toluene, and then 0.02 g of AIBN was added as the initiator. Subsequently, the reaction was mechanically stirring at 110 °C for 24 h under N2 atmosphere. After the completion of the reaction, the Sil-ImC18/CDs was washed with methanol, ethanol, distilled water, and ethanol separately, dried at 60 °C before packing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18 stationary phases
ce:section.ce:para: 0.8 g of ImC18CDs and 2.7 g of Sil-MPS were dispersed into 20 mL anhydrous toluene. Then the same experimental procedure was used as the previous section. Finally, the Sil-ImC18CDs stationary phase was obtained. The preparation of the Sil-ImC18 stationary phase was similar to Sil-ImC18CDs, the difference is that 0.8 g of ImC18CDs was replaced by 2.7 g of [C18VIm]Br.

Firstly,2.0 g of Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18/CDs, and Sil-ImC18CDs stationary phases were dispersed into 21 mL of carbon tetrachloride, respectively. Then, the suspension was added into a stainless-steel column (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.), which were downward packed with 40 MPa packing press and methanol as the propelling solvent.

4.0 g of Radix Astragali powder (passed through 65-mesh sieve) was added into 50 mL methanol and ultrasonication for 1 h, Then, stay it overnight. the filtrate was collected through filtering after ultrasonic extraction at 30 °C for 1 h. Finally, the extraction was dissolved in 5 mL methanol and filtered before use.

A variety of characterization methods were used to verify the successful preparation of ImC18CDs, Sil-ImC18CDs, Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18/CDs. The morphology of ImC18CDs was observed by TEM (Fig. 2 a), some of the carbon dots are marked in red circles, and their particle size was measured by TEM, and the size is about 3 nm. It can be observed that compared with bare silica, the Sil-ImC18/CDs exhibited rough appearance with a thickness of about 150 nm, which indicates that the ImC18CDs and their precursor were successfully co-grafted on the silica surface (Fig. S1). According to the fluorescence characteristics of ImC18CDs, the modification of ImC18CDs on the surface of silica was verified by LSCM, it can be seen from Fig. 2b and Fig. S2 that Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18/CDs show blue fluorescence.

Elemental analysis results of ImC18CDs, Sil-MPS, Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18/CDs are shown in Table 1 . Compared to Sil-MPS, the C, H and N contents of Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18/CDs were increased significantly. It indicates that ImC18CDs and [C18VIm]Br are successfully bonded to the surface of silica. Through the calculation of formula (1) [33], the bonding density of Sil-ImC18/CDs (in total) can reach 1.96 μmol m−2.
(1)
B o n d i n g
a m o u n t
( μ m o l / m 2 )
= N % ×
10 6
/
(
M N
× S )
N% refers to the percentage of nitrogen content, MN stands for the molar mass of nitrogen, and S represents the surface area of bare silica.

Contact angle experiments are used to verify the hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of stationary phase materials. Firstly, the sample was screened (250 mesh) and then immobilized on a flat glass substrate. Subsequently, 10 μL water is dropped on the screened sample surface, and the hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of the material are judged by observing the angle between the material and the water drop θ by photoelectric microscope. The θ value of Sil-MPS is 75.4° (Fig. 2c), which indicates that it has a hydrophilic surface (θ < 90°). The θ value of Sil-ImC18CDs is 125.9° (Fig. S3a), which exhibits obvious hydrophobic properties (θ > 90°) and indicates most of the exposed silicon hydroxyl groups on the surface of Sil-ImC18CDs lead to poor hydrophobicity. The θ values of Sil-ImC18 and Sil-ImC18/CDs are 158.9° (Fig. S3b) and 170.1° (Fig. 2d), respectively, which are typical superhydrophobic surfaces (θ > 150°), because Sil-ImC18 contains octadecyl chain and has strong hydrophobicity, [C18VIm]Br and ImC18CDs enhance the hydrophobicity of Sil-ImC18/CDs due to their synergistic effect. The above experimental results directly present that the modification of silica was successful. In addition, the zeta potentials of silica and Sil-ImC18/CDs were measured. As displayed in Fig. S4, the bare silica shows an average zeta-potential of −5.29 mV, revealing that negative charges were exposed on the bare silica surface. After silica was modified with ImC18CDs and their precursor, this value increased to 23.77 mV because the ImC18CDs and their precursor can influence and change the surface charge of silica.

The FT-IR spectra of the [C18VIm]Br, ImC18CDs, Sil-MPS, Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18/CDs are displayed in Fig. S5. In all the spectra, the absorption peak at 1110 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of siloxane (Si–O) groups of silica, 1460 cm−1 is the flexural stretching vibration peak of –CH2, 2855 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of –CH2, 3430 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration peaks of O–H and N–H. 1660 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic peak of CC in the spectrum of ImC18CDs, the CC absorption peak of Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18/CDs appear at 1628 cm−1, indicating that ImC18CDs has a blue shift maybe due to the polarity of the solvent during the process of bonding to the silica surface. Compared with the spectrum of Sil-MPS, the peak intensity of Sil-ImC18CDs at 2925 cm−1, 2855 cm−1,1628 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1 are not significantly change, while the increase of the bonding amount of Sil-ImC18/CDs results in a significant increase in the peak intensity at these four places, and it proved that ImC18CDs and [C18VIm]Br were successfully modified on the surface of silica. Besides, compared with the spectrum of Sil-ImC18CDs, 1567 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of benzene ring in the spectrum of Sil-ImC18/CDs, it further illustrates the successful preparation of the Sil-ImC18/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SRM870 and SRM869b standard test mixture
ce:section.ce:para: The SRM870 standard test mixture includes five compounds: uracil, toluene, ethylbenzene, amitriptyline and 1,4-dihydroxyanthraquinone. Amitriptyline is a basic compound commonly used by column for detection of silanol activity. Elution of amitriptyline with symmetrical peak shape is often associated with high silanol activity. Because both Sil-ImC18/CDs and commercial C18 column (Agilent ZORBAX SB-C18) contain octadecyl chains, and the silica with low acid in ZORBAX SB-C18 has excellent peak shape for acidic, alkaline and neutral compounds, and therefore ZORBAX SB-C18 is chosen as the contrast commercial column in this experiment. Among which, the polar functional groups in the Sil-ImC18/CDs column has a certain shielding effect on silanol groups, so that the activity of silanol groups is low. However, ZORBAX SB-C18 has high silanol activity. Generally, 1,4-dihydroxyanthraquinone is eluted after ethylbenzene and before amitriptyline hydrochloride. The elution sequence of Sil-ImC18/CDs is opposite to that of the commercial C18 stationary phase (Fig. S6a). The results show that Sil-ImC18/CDs has not only low silicon hydroxyl activity and metal activity, but also embedded polar functionality, compared with the commercial C18 column [34]. Among them, the peak asymmetry factor of Sil-ImC18/CDs for 1,4-dihydroxyanthraquinone is similar to that of commercial C18 stationary phase. This factor is proportional to the metal content of silica [35]. It can be seen that the purity of the silica sphere prepared by Sil-ImC18/CDs and commercial C18 are low and the metal content is relatively high.

The SRM869b standard test mixture is composed of phenanthrene (Phe), tetrabenzonaphthalene (TBN) and benzo[a]pyrene (BaP). Further evidence for its shape selectivity is acquired by separation of SRM869b, where in contrast to the case of ZORBAX SB-C18, selectivity factor for TBN and BaP is 2.05, smaller than ZORBAX SB-C18 (Fig. S6b), which can be seen that the shape selectivity of the Sil-ImC18/CDs column is stronger.

Tanaka standard test mixture is composed by seven compounds included uracil, caffeine, phenol, n-butylbenzene, n-pentylbenzene, o-terphenyl and triphenylene. The hydrogen bonding capacity, hydrophobicity or hydrophobic selectivity and shape selectivity of the chromatographic stationary phase can be explained by the retention behavior of the Tanaka standard test mixture [36,37]. The separation of Tanaka test mixture on Sil-ImC18/CDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns is shown in Fig. 3 a. Among which, the surface coverage and surface area of the stationary phase can be determined by the retention factor of n-pentylbenzene (k PB). In general, with the increase of k PB value, the surface area and surface coverage are enhanced. The k PB of Sil-ImC18/CDs is 4.39, and the k PB of ZORBAX SB-C18 is 6.84, which may due to that the surface coverage and surface area of Sil-ImC18/CDs are slightly smaller than those of ZORBAX SB-C18 column. The separation factor (α CH2 = k PB/k BB) of n-pentylbenzene and n-butylbenzene is 1.72, which indicates that the stationary phase has certain hydrophobic selectivity. The retention factor ratio between triphenylene and o-terphenyl, α T/O = k T/k O is 1.80, which indicates that the Sil-ImC18/CDs has certain shape selectivity. The separation factor ratio (α C/P = kc/kp) of caffeine and phenol is 0.26, which represents that the silanol groups on the surface of the Sil-ImC18/CDs can form hydrogen bonds with caffeine has low activity, and some silanol groups may be hidden by ImC18CDs.

In 1990, a method using the mixture of thiourea, toluene, ethylbenzene, aniline, phenol and p-toluidine as analytes is invented by Engelhardt et al. [38] to test the hydrophobicity and silanol activity of chromatographic columns. According to the literature [38], methanol-water (55/45, v/v) is the optimal mobile phase condition for evaluating C8 and C18 columns. When the water content is less than 50%, the interaction between the bonded alkyl chain and eluting components is stronger than their own. In general, the relative retention of toluene and ethylbenzene are used to evaluate the hydrophobicity of the stationary phase, and the relative retention of aniline and phenol are used to evaluate the activity of silanol groups. The separation effect of Engelhardt standard test mixture on Sil-ImC18/CDs chromatographic column and ZORBAX SB-C18 commercial column is shown in Fig. 3b. The separation factor of toluene and ethylbenzene is 2.26, which indicates that the Sil-ImC18/CDs column has good hydrophobic selectivity. The separation factor of aniline and phenol is 4.79, and aniline is eluted before phenol, which further shown that the Sil-ImC18/CDs column has low silanol activity.
The separation parameter values of Sil-ImC18/CDs and ZORBAX SB-C18 columns were listed in Table S1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of amines, phenols, PAHs and alkylbenzenes
ce:section.ce:para: The separation effect of five aromatic amines, six PAHs and six alkylbenzenes are shown in Fig. S7. It can be seen from the figure that Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18CDs, and Sil-ImC18/CDs have similar retention capacity for the above types of substances, and the elution order is also the same. The most prominent is that Sil-ImC18/CDs achieved the basic separation of analytes in short retention time, and has better peak shape. The reason for this is that there is a synergistic effect between [C18VIm]Br and ImC18CDs, which co-bonding on the surface of silica enhances the selectivity of Sil-ImC18/CDs.

Then, eight phenols including p-aminophenol, hydroquinone, 2-aminophenol, phenol, p-cresol, 2-acetylaminophenol, 2-tert-butylphenol, naphthol were chosen to evaluate the retention behavior in RPLC mode. Compared with the ZORBAX SB-C18, the Sil-ImC18/CDs column shows stronger retention capacity for phenols under the same chromatographic conditions, which may be due to stronger interactions such as π-π and hydrogen-bonding between imidazolium and phenols, thereby achieving the basic separation of eight phenols (Fig. 3c).

The butyl benzene isomers (tert-butylbenzene, sec-butylbenzene, iso-butylbenzene, n-butylbenzene), tetracyclic PAHs (fluoranthene, pyrene, chrysene, 1,2-benzoanthracene) and tricyclic PAHs (acenaphthene, fluorene, phenanthrene, anthracene) were chosen to further investigate the separation ability of isomers of our prepared column. The elution sequence of all these analytes on the four chromatographic columns (Sil-ImC18, Sil-ImC18CDs, Sil-ImC18/CDs and ZORBAX SB-C18) are identical (Fig. 4 ), but the separation performance is quite different. Among them, Sil-ImC18/CDs achieved baseline separation of fluoranthene/pyrene, phenanthrene/anthracene, acenaphthene/fluorene, 1,2-benzoanthracene/chrysene (the most difficult to separate among 1647e standard test mixture [35]). Fluoranthene/pyrene is not separated on ZORBAX SB-C18 column, which may be due to the stronger interaction between C18 stationary phase and high lipophilic analytes. In addition, because the insufficient purity of the butylbenzene isomers and certain impurities, solvent peaks and other impurity peaks appeared when the retention time was ≤5 min. However, there was no obvious impurity peak in Sil-ImC18CDs and Sil-ImC18 columns, which may be due to the short retention time and poor separation selectivity. To sum up, the good aromatic and hydrophobic selectivity of Sil-ImC18/CDs are due to the π-π interaction between imidazole cation and π electron aromatic ring, as well as strong hydrophobic interaction of octadecyl chains in Sil-ImC18/CDs.

In addition, the retention behavior and selectivity of analytes are not only related to the types of chromatographic stationary phases, but also closely related to chromatographic conditions. It is very important to select the optimal experimental conditions for the separation and detection of analytes. Therefore, the effects of different volume fractions of methanol in the mobile phase and different temperatures on the retention time of six PAHs on the Sil-ImC18/CDs column were further studied, as shown in Fig. S8a. With the increase of methanol content, the retention time decreased gradually. From this, it can be seen that there may be π-π interaction and hydrophobic interaction between the PAHs and Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase. When the temperature rises in the range of 20–40 °C, the ln k of the analytes decreases linearly (Fig. S8b). The linear change of ln k with temperature is related to the constant relative rate [39], and may be related to the stable form of the bonded substance.

In order to investigate the stability of Sil-ImC18/CDs column, eight phenols were used as testing samples, under the same chromatographic conditions injecting ten times continuously. The results are presented in Fig. S9, the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of the retention time of eight phenols in the Sil-ImC18/CDs were calculated within 0.51–3.03% (n = 10), which proved that Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase has good stability.

In order to investigate the repeatability of Sil-ImC18/CDs, different batches of Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase were prepared by the same raw material and the same synthesis method. As listed in Table S2, the C, N and H contents of Batch 1 and 2 are basically the same, indicating that the stationary phase has good repeatability. In addition, two aromatic acids were selected as the analytes. Under the same chromatographic conditions, the mixed samples of the two aromatic acids were separated on different batches of Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase. The chromatogram is shown in Fig. S10. The RSD results of the two aromatic acids were 0.14 and 3.19%, which further indicates that the Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase has good repeatability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of flavonoids in Radix Astragali extract solution
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of standard solution of the flavonoids including calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin was configured among 0.1–1.0 mg mL−1. According to the standard curves (Fig. 5 a), the values of R2 were found to be 0.9994, 0.9985, 0.9999 and 0.9980, respectively. Subsequently, the Sil-ImC18/CDs column was applied to separate and quantify the calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin from Radix Astragali extract solution. The contents of calycosin-7-glucoside, ononin, calycosin and formononetin in Radix Astragali extract were calculated to be 0.25 mg mL−1, 0.15 mg mL−1, 0.13 mg mL−1 and 0.30 mg mL−1 (Fig. 5b).

1-Vinyl-3-octadecylimidazolium bromide ILs and their derived carbon dots were co-immobilized on silica and applied as new hydrophobic chromatographic stationary phases. The synergistic effect of ImC18CDs and [C18VIm]Br enhanced the separation performance of Sil-ImC18/CDs in RPLC, and its separation effect was better than that of Sil-ImC18CDs, Sil-ImC18 and commercial C18 columns. Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase was good at separating alkylbenzene, PAHs, aromatic amines, and phenols. The hydrophobicity, aromatic selectivity, and shape selectivity of Sil-ImC18/CDs were investigated by Tanaka, Engelhardt, SRM870 and SRM869b. The certain shape selectivity of Sil-ImC18/CDs for PAH isomers is due to the strong hydrophobic interaction of octadecyl chains and the π-π stacking effect provided by imidazolium. In addition, the quantitative determination of ononin, calycosin-7-glucoside, formononetin and calycosin in the extract of Radix Astragali was realized by Sil-ImC18/CDs. Based on the above research, Sil-ImC18/CDs stationary phase could be used for the selective separation of multiple components, which not only enriched the types and scope of multifunctional separation materials, but also referenced for the design of reversed-phase chromatography stationary phases significantly.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22074154 and 21822407), Science and Technology Project of Science and Technology Bureau of Chengguan District in Lanzhou City (2020JSCX0033), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2021420) and the Foundation for Sci & Tech Research Project of Gansu Province (20JR10RA045 and 20JR5RA573).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122513.

Danni Jiang: Experiment, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Jia Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Reviewing and Editing. Ming Guan: Methodology. Hongdeng Qiu: Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
